title: Cat Eyelid Twitching: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your cat's eyelid twitching? Learn about blepharospasm, why it is usually a low-urgency issue, and how photos help your vet provide better care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Eyelid twitching in cats, or blepharospasm, is often a minor muscle tic due to irritation or stress, but requires veterinary attention if accompanied by redness, discharge, or persistent pawing.

What is eyelid twitching in cats and what are the common signs?

Known medically as blepharospasm, eyelid twitching in cats is an involuntary contraction of the muscles surrounding the eye. It can range from a subtle flicker to a more pronounced squint. Often, this is a symptom of mild irritation, such as a dust particle, or it could be related to stress or local nerve sensitivity.

Is my cat's eyelid twitching considered a medical emergency?

  • Monitor your cat at home for a day or two if they are acting normally, eating, and playing.
  • Schedule a non-emergency veterinary visit if you notice significant redness or heavy discharge.
  • Seek professional care if your cat is pawing at their eye excessively to rule out serious issues like corneal ulcers.

How does a photo or video help my veterinarian triage eyelid twitching?

  • Captures intermittent twitching that may not occur during the actual veterinary appointment.
  • Allows the veterinary team to see the exact frequency and nature of the twitching.
  • Helps the vet distinguish between a simple muscle tic and structural issues with the eyelid or eyeball itself.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Eyelid twitching in cats, while not directly addressed in the provided excerpts, can be associated with several underlying conditions. Neurologic signs, potentially related to middle or inner ear involvement from tumors, may manifest as facial nerve palsy, characterized by facial drooping, and Horner syndrome, indicated by eyelid droop. Systemic diseases, such as feline herpesvirus-1, can cause conjunctivitis. While eyelid and conjunctival tumors are relatively frequent ophthalmic neoplasms in cats, particularly squamous cell carcinomas in white cats with nonpigmented eyelid margins, these typically present as pink, roughened masses rather than twitching. Any persistent or unusual ocular signs warrant veterinary examination to determine the underlying cause and appropriate treatment.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Infectious Disease, Dermatology, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 505)

faqs.question: Can stress cause my cat's eye to twitch?
faqs.answer: Yes, stress or fatigue can occasionally lead to minor muscle tremors around the eyes, though physical irritation or environmental allergens are more common causes.

faqs.question: How long should I wait before calling a vet?
faqs.answer: If the twitching persists for more than 24 to 48 hours, or if it is accompanied by swelling or cloudiness in the eye, it is best to consult your veterinarian for an exam.
